question: Explain the mechanism whereby anti-CTLA-4 monoclonal antibodies (e.g., ipilimumab) unleash a tumor-specific T-cell response while simultaneously causing immune-related adverse events (irAEs), and compare this to the mechanistic profile of anti-PD-1/PD-L1 agents.
answer:
Anti-CTLA-4 blockade unleashes naïve T-cell priming in the lymph node by removing the dominant negative signal delivered when CTLA-4, upregulated on activated T-cells, binds B7-1/2 with higher avidity than CD28; ipilimumab thereby increases the ratio of effector-to-regulatory T-cells (Teff/Treg) that traffic to and infiltrate the tumor.  CTLA-4 is constitutively expressed on FoxP3+ Tregs, so additional Treg suppression is relieved, further shifting the Teff/Treg balance.  These polyclonal, low-affinity T-cells can cross-react with shared antigens expressed on normal tissues (e.g., melan-A in skin or pituitary gland), driving irAEs such as dermatitis, colitis, and hypophysitis via cytokine release and direct cytotoxicity.  In contrast, anti-PD-1/PD-L1 antibodies act primarily in the tumor microenvironment and draining lymph nodes where PD-1 on exhausted CD8+ T-cells encounters PD-L1 upregulated on tumor cells and myeloid-derived suppressor cells.  PD-1 ligation attenuates TCR and PI3K-Akt-mTOR signaling, reduces T-cell motility, and promotes the expression of co-inhibitory modules (e.g., TIGIT, LAG-3).  Checkpoint reversal by nivolumab or pembrolizumab restores TCR-proximal ZAP70, PLC-γ1 phosphorylation, granzyme B synthesis, and metabolic fitness without broad systemic T-cell activation.  Consequently, anti-PD-1/PD-L1 therapy generates fewer high-grade irAEs (<20 % grade ≥3) but achieves lower objective response rates (~20–40 %) compared with ipilimumab-based regimens, necessitating combination approaches such as ipilimumab+nivolumab which yield 50–60 % responses albeit at the cost of 50–60 % grade ≥3 toxicity.  Recent trials (CheckMate-067, CheckMate-227) confirm that dual checkpoint blockade increases 5-year OS to ~40 % in metastatic melanoma but demands rigorous endocrine, dermatologic, and hepatic monitoring.  In summary, CTLA-4 blockade acts as a systemic immune accelerator with broad tissue involvement, whereas PD-1/PD-L1 blockade is a targeted microenvironmental intervention that preserves systemic tolerance.

Summary:  CTLA-4 blockade removes a global brake on T-cell priming and Treg function, driving durable tumor immunity but frequent irAEs; PD-1/PD-L1 blockade reactivates pre-existing exhausted T-cells within the tumor niche with fewer off-tumor effects, yielding safer but sometimes less potent responses.
